title: Reader Type Selector Guide
summary: Use this guide to choose reader technology based on security level, credential preference, environment, throughput, and panel interface before the design moves into lock power and panel planning.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: Reader Type Selector helps choose a practical reader direction after the door’s fail-state behavior has already been reviewed.
The tool compares security expectations, credential preference, environmental exposure, user flow, and panel interface to produce a planning recommendation.

It is not a vendor compatibility matrix. It is a design sanity check that helps prevent reader selection from being made in isolation from credentials, wiring, environment, and long-term migration needs.

sections.title: When to use it
bullets: After deciding fail-safe, fail-secure, or conditional behavior for the opening.
Before ordering readers for a new install, expansion, or migration.
When deciding whether legacy Wiegand readers are still acceptable.
When comparing card/fob, mobile, keypad, or multi-factor reader strategies.
When a door needs outdoor, harsh-environment, high-throughput, or hands-free behavior.
When documenting why one opening needs a stronger reader than another.

links.label: Fail-Safe / Fail-Secure guide
links.note: Use this related guide when reader choice depends on how the opening should behave during power loss.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These headings match the visible input labels on the calculator so the guide can be read directly against the form.

items.label: Security Level
items.description: The required assurance level for the opening. This reflects how much credential integrity, reader supervision, encryption, and authentication strength matter for the door.
examples: Low: general interior office or low-risk controlled area.
Medium: normal business access where encrypted credentials are preferred.
High: critical spaces, sensitive areas, exterior doors, IT rooms, or locations where cloning or credential misuse would be a serious concern.
items.whyItMatters: Higher security pressure should push the design toward stronger credentials, supervised communication, and possibly multi-factor authentication.
items.commonMistake: Using the same basic reader everywhere because it is simple to stock, even when some openings have very different risk profiles.

links.label: Credential Format Helper guide
links.note: Use this related guide when security level also depends on credential format, bit length, numbering space, or migration pressure.

items.label: Credential Preference
items.description: The preferred way users authenticate at the reader. This can be a physical card/fob, mobile credential, keypad/PIN, or multi-factor approach.
examples: Card / Fob: common physical credential workflow.
PIN / keypad: code entry, often used where cards are not ideal or as a secondary factor.
Multi-factor: more than one authentication method for higher-assurance checkpoints.
items.whyItMatters: Credential preference affects reader hardware, user training, enrollment process, support calls, and future migration flexibility.
items.commonMistake: Choosing a credential style based only on user convenience without checking reader compatibility, security policy, and lifecycle support.

links.label: Credential Format Helper guide
links.note: Use this related guide when card/fob decisions depend on format capacity, numbering headroom, or migration planning.

items.label: Environment
items.description: The physical conditions where the reader will be installed. Indoor, outdoor, and harsh locations create different durability and rating requirements.
examples: Indoor: conditioned office, corridor, lobby, or controlled interior space.
Outdoor: exterior wall, gate, parking entrance, or weather-exposed location.
Harsh: industrial, dusty, wet, corrosive, impact-prone, or temperature-stressed location.
items.whyItMatters: Reader survivability depends on enclosure rating, temperature range, UV/weather exposure, vandal resistance, mounting method, and maintenance access.
items.commonMistake: Installing an indoor-rated reader outside or in a harsh environment because it works during bench testing.

items.label: Throughput
items.description: How quickly users need to move through the opening. This affects whether a standard wall reader is acceptable or whether faster, mobile, hands-free, or long-range behavior is needed.
examples: Standard: normal user flow with card tap or mobile presentation.
High Throughput: busy doors, shift changes, lobbies, turnstiles, or high-volume employee entrances.
Hands-free: vehicle gates, accessibility-driven use cases, or locations where stopping to present a card is undesirable.
items.whyItMatters: Reader choice can create bottlenecks. A secure reader that slows down a high-volume entrance may cause tailgating, user frustration, or bypass behavior.
items.commonMistake: Optimizing only for security strength and ignoring how users actually move through the opening during peak periods.

items.label: Panel Interface
items.description: The communication method between the reader and the access-control panel. The tool compares legacy Wiegand against OSDP-style supervised communication.
examples: Wiegand: common legacy signaling, simple and widely supported, but not encrypted or supervised in the same way.
OSDP: stronger modern option when supported by both reader and panel, with better supervision and security features.
items.whyItMatters: Panel interface affects wiring, supervision, encryption, tamper visibility, migration options, and whether the reader selection locks the project into weaker legacy behavior.
items.commonMistake: Defaulting to Wiegand on new deployments even when the panel and reader can support OSDP.

links.label: Lock Power Budget guide
links.note: Use this related guide when reader/interface planning also affects power supply layout, lock current, or future door hardware wiring.

sections.title: How to interpret results
body: The recommendation is a reader planning direction. It should be validated against the actual access-control platform, supported credentials, reader model, panel interface, and site policy.

The result combines the selected security level, credential preference, environment, throughput, and panel interface into a practical recommendation for reader type, interface direction, environmental rating, and user-flow behavior.
bullets: Reader Type: the recommended reader family or authentication style.
Interface: whether the selected interface is acceptable or should be reconsidered.
Security: the expected credential/security posture.
Environment: whether indoor, outdoor, or harsher-rated hardware should be considered.
Throughput: whether user flow should influence reader style.
Actionable Guidance: the practical next step before locking the design.

sections.title: Healthy / Watch / Risk interpretation
body: This tool uses status to show whether the reader direction is reasonably aligned with the entered assumptions.
A HEALTHY result means the selected choices are generally consistent. A WATCH result means security level, interface, or credential choice deserves review before the design moves forward.
bullets: HEALTHY: reader choice and assumptions are reasonably aligned.
WATCH: review the interface, credential type, or security level before ordering hardware.
A high-security opening with Wiegand or non-MFA assumptions should be treated carefully, even if the hardware appears compatible.

sections.title: Reader selection workflow
bullets: Start from the fail-state decision so the reader choice matches the actual door behavior.
Set the security level based on risk, not convenience.
Choose credential preference based on site policy, user workflow, and support model.
Check environment before assuming a standard indoor reader will survive.
Check throughput so the reader does not create a bottleneck or encourage bypass behavior.
Prefer stronger supervised interfaces such as OSDP when supported by the panel and reader.
Document the assumption before moving into lock power and panel capacity planning.

links.label: Fail-Safe / Fail-Secure guide
links.note: Use this related guide when the reader decision depends on the opening’s fail behavior.

links.label: Lock Power Budget guide
links.note: Use this related guide when reader and lock choices need to be carried into power supply planning.

sections.title: OSDP vs Wiegand planning notes
body: Wiegand is widely deployed and may be necessary for legacy systems, but it is a weaker default for new work when stronger supervised communication is available.
OSDP is usually the stronger planning direction when both reader and panel support it, especially for higher-security openings or new deployments.
The decision should still be verified against controller support, reader addressing, wiring topology, cable distance, firmware compatibility, and the site’s standard.
bullets: Use Wiegand carefully when legacy support is required.
Prefer OSDP where secure supervision and modern reader management matter.
Do not assume an OSDP-capable reader solves every compatibility issue by itself.
Document the interface choice because it affects wiring, troubleshooting, and future migration.

sections.title: Migration notes
body: Reader selection can either make a future credential migration easier or lock the site deeper into a legacy path.
Dual-technology readers, mobile-ready readers, and OSDP-capable readers can help create a cleaner upgrade path, but only when the platform and credential strategy are planned together.
bullets: Confirm whether existing cards must keep working during the transition.
Check whether the new reader supports both old and new credential types.
Plan whether mobile credentials will be enabled now or reserved for later.
Avoid buying single-purpose readers if the site is likely to migrate soon.
Coordinate reader replacement with credential format and enrollment process.

links.label: Credential Format Helper guide
links.note: Use this related guide when reader migration is tied to credential format, numbering capacity, or badge-population growth.

sections.title: Red flags
bullets: High security level is paired with Wiegand by default.
The site wants mobile credentials but has not confirmed platform licensing, app support, or reader compatibility.
Outdoor or harsh readers are selected using indoor hardware assumptions.
A busy entrance is assigned a slow or inconvenient authentication workflow.
A high-security door relies on single-factor card-only behavior without review.
Reader technology is chosen before credential format and panel interface are confirmed.
The project assumes old badges, new badges, and mobile credentials will all work without a migration plan.

sections.title: Documentation checklist
bullets: Security Level and reason for that classification.
Credential Preference and supported credential types.
Environment rating assumptions.
Throughput requirement and peak-use condition.
Panel Interface decision: Wiegand or OSDP.
Reader type recommendation.
Compatibility assumptions for panel, reader, firmware, credential, and software.
Migration plan if replacing legacy readers or credentials.
Any exceptions where one door needs a different reader standard than the rest.

sections.title: Common mistakes
bullets: Buying readers before credential strategy is standardized.
Using legacy proximity credentials everywhere because they are familiar.
Treating Wiegand as the default on new systems without reviewing OSDP support.
Ignoring environmental ratings until readers fail outdoors.
Choosing multi-factor or mobile readers without planning enrollment and user support.
Optimizing only for security while ignoring throughput and user behavior.
Assuming a reader works with every panel, credential, and software version just because the form factor looks right.

sections.title: Assumptions and limits
bullets: This tool is a planning selector, not a vendor-specific compatibility matrix.
Reader recommendations must be verified against the selected access-control platform.
Interface recommendations assume the panel and reader support the selected signaling method.
Outdoor and harsh-environment recommendations should be verified against manufacturer environmental ratings.
Credential and authentication choices should be aligned with site security policy, user workflow, lifecycle support, and manufacturer documentation.
